course_info.title: Content Creation and Storytelling
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 4 hours per week

class_schedule.topic: The Creator Economy and Content as Communication
learning_objectives: Define the creator economy and its impact on professional communications
Distinguish between content marketing, brand journalism, and personal branding content
Identify the skills required to function as a professional content creator
readings: Harvard Business Review, 'The Creator Economy Explained'

lecture_notes.introduction: Content creation is no longer a hobby or side function. It is a core competency for organizations and individuals in the digital communications landscape. This course develops you into a skilled content creator who can plan, produce, and distribute compelling content that serves strategic objectives.
key_concepts: The creator economy: the $100B+ ecosystem of individuals and organizations producing content as a primary communication and business strategy
Content marketing vs. advertising: earning attention through value rather than buying it through interruption
Brand journalism: applying journalistic storytelling techniques to organizational communication
Content creation as a career: roles, specializations, and career paths in professional content creation
The content creation workflow: ideate, plan, produce, edit, optimize, distribute, measure, iterate
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study analysis of three content creators who built significant audiences: one corporate brand, one nonprofit, and one individual, examining their content strategy, production quality, and audience growth
setup_requirements: Portfolio platform account (Behance, Contently, or personal website)
Content creation toolkit: smartphone, laptop, Canva, CapCut

review.value: A strategic approach to creating and distributing valuable, relevant, and consistent content to attract and retain a clearly defined audience, ultimately driving profitable customer action without direct selling

The repeatable process of ideation, planning, production, editing, optimization, distribution, measurement, and iteration that transforms content creation from random acts of posting into a systematic practice

learning_objectives: Apply the hero's journey structure to brand and organizational storytelling
Use the AIDA framework to structure content for conversion
Adapt storytelling frameworks for different content lengths and formats

lecture_notes.introduction: Every piece of content is a story, whether you intend it to be or not. The question is whether you are telling it deliberately and effectively. Storytelling frameworks give you repeatable structures that make content compelling without reinventing the wheel each time.
key_concepts: The hero's journey condensed: status quo, disruption, struggle, transformation, new normal
AIDA framework: Attention, Interest, Desire, Action as a content conversion structure
PAS framework: Problem, Agitation, Solution for persuasive content
Before-After-Bridge: showing the gap between current state and desired state, then presenting the bridge
Micro-stories: applying narrative structure to captions, tweets, and 60-second videos
lecture_notes.demonstration: Deconstructing a viral Instagram Reel, a high-performing LinkedIn post, and a successful email campaign to identify which storytelling framework each uses and how the framework drives engagement
setup_requirements: Storytelling framework reference cards (provided on LMS)

review.value: A content structure progressing through Attention (hook), Interest (relevant details), Desire (emotional connection to outcome), and Action (clear CTA), commonly used for marketing and persuasive content
Problem (name the pain point), Agitation (explore consequences of inaction), Solution (present your offering as the answer), effective for sales-oriented content and landing pages

class_schedule.topic: Visual Storytelling and Branded Content Design
learning_objectives: Develop a visual brand system for consistent content creation
Create branded templates for recurring content formats
Apply visual storytelling techniques to social media carousels and infographics
readings: Canva Design School, 'Brand Kit and Templates' module

lecture_notes.introduction: Visual consistency builds brand recognition. When your audience can identify your content in a crowded feed before reading a single word, you have achieved visual brand equity. Templates and brand systems make this achievable even for solo creators.
key_concepts: Visual brand system: logo usage, color palette, typography, image style, layout patterns
Template creation: designing reusable formats for quotes, tips, carousels, and announcements
Carousel storytelling: using the swipe mechanic to build narrative tension across slides
Infographic design: transforming data and processes into visual stories
Photography and illustration style: maintaining visual consistency across all content
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a complete visual brand kit and three content templates in Canva for a fictional startup, then creating a 10-slide educational carousel using the templates
setup_requirements: Canva Pro (free trial or student account)
Brand brief for exercise (provided on LMS)

review.value: The recognition value built through consistent visual presentation; audiences should be able to identify your content by its visual style alone, reducing dependence on logo placement for brand attribution

Using the multi-slide format to create a narrative arc: hook on slide 1, build tension through the middle slides, deliver the payoff or CTA on the final slide, leveraging the swipe mechanic for pacing

class_schedule.topic: Video Content Creation for Social Platforms
learning_objectives: Plan and produce short-form video content optimized for social platforms
Apply hook, body, CTA structure to video scripts
Edit video with platform-native tools and export for multiple platforms simultaneously
readings: Stockman, How to Shoot Video That Doesn't Suck, Ch. 6-10
TikTok for Business, 'Creative Best Practices' guide

lecture_notes.introduction: Short-form video is the dominant content format across every major social platform. The ability to plan, shoot, and edit compelling video content quickly is the single most valuable production skill for a digital communicator today.
key_concepts: The 3-second hook: capturing attention before the scroll, using visual surprise, bold text, or provocative opening lines
Script structure for short video: hook (0-3s), context (3-10s), body (10-45s), CTA (final 5s)
B-roll and cutaway: breaking up talking-head footage with supplementary visuals
Text overlays and captions: designing for sound-off viewing, which is the default on most feeds
Multi-platform export: creating vertical, horizontal, and square versions from one shoot
lecture_notes.demonstration: Filming three 30-second videos in class: one educational tip, one behind-the-scenes, and one testimonial format, then editing all three with text overlays and music in CapCut
setup_requirements: Ring light or window light setup

review.value: The opening moment of a video that must capture attention before the viewer scrolls; effective hooks use visual disruption, bold text statements, unexpected actions, or direct questions
Designing video content to be understood without audio, using text overlays, captions, and visual storytelling, since 85% of social media video is watched without sound

class_schedule.topic: Long-Form Content: Blogs, Articles, and Newsletters
learning_objectives: Structure long-form digital content for readability and SEO performance
Develop a content series strategy that builds audience over time
Apply content atomization to extract multiple short-form pieces from one long-form asset
readings: Substack, 'How to Write a Great Newsletter' guide

lecture_notes.introduction: While short-form content captures attention, long-form content builds authority, trust, and search visibility. The most effective content strategies combine both, with long-form assets as the foundation and short-form content as the distribution layer.
key_concepts: Pillar content strategy: creating comprehensive long-form pieces that serve as the authoritative hub for a topic
Blog post structures: listicle, how-to guide, case study, opinion/analysis, roundup
Newsletter differentiation: what makes someone subscribe and stay subscribed
Content atomization: systematically breaking one long-form piece into 10-20 short-form assets
Evergreen vs. timely content: balancing content that endures with content that captures the moment
lecture_notes.demonstration: Taking a 2000-word blog post and live-atomizing it into an Instagram carousel, three Twitter/X posts, a LinkedIn article teaser, an email newsletter feature, and a 60-second video script
setup_requirements: Sample long-form content for atomization exercise

review.value: A comprehensive, authoritative long-form piece covering a broad topic, designed to rank in search and serve as a hub that links to and from more specific subtopic content
The systematic process of breaking one substantial content asset into many smaller platform-specific pieces, maximizing the return on creative investment while ensuring each piece stands alone

learning_objectives: Develop a distribution strategy that extends content reach beyond organic posting
Identify appropriate earned, owned, and paid distribution channels for different content types
Apply content seeding and influencer amplification techniques ethically

lecture_notes.introduction: Creating great content is only half the job. Without deliberate distribution, even exceptional content disappears into the void. The best content creators spend as much time distributing content as they do creating it.
key_concepts: The 80/20 distribution rule: spend 20% of effort creating and 80% distributing
Owned channels: website, email list, social profiles, app notifications
Earned channels: media coverage, social sharing, backlinks, word of mouth
Paid channels: social ads, sponsored content, influencer partnerships, search ads
Content seeding: strategically sharing content with key individuals and communities before or during launch
lecture_notes.demonstration: Developing a complete distribution plan for a single blog post: owned channel posting schedule, earned media outreach list, paid promotion budget allocation, and content seeding targets
setup_requirements: Sample content piece for distribution planning exercise

review.value: Strategically sharing content with influential individuals, community leaders, and relevant online communities at launch to generate early engagement and social proof that triggers algorithmic amplification

Owned media (your channels), earned media (others sharing your content organically), and paid media (amplification through advertising) form the distribution triad; effective strategies use all three in coordination

questions.question: Compare the AIDA and PAS storytelling frameworks. For each, describe its structure and give one content format where it is most effective.
questions.answer: AIDA (Attention, Interest, Desire, Action) progressively builds from capturing attention to creating interest through details, stimulating desire through emotional connection, and ending with a clear call to action. It is most effective in email marketing campaigns or landing pages where the reader progresses through a journey from awareness to conversion. PAS (Problem, Agitation, Solution) names a pain point, explores the consequences of leaving it unaddressed, then presents the solution. It is most effective in sales-oriented social media posts or ad copy where the goal is to create urgency around a specific problem.

rubric.key: Identifies appropriate format for each with reasoning

questions.question: Explain why visual brand consistency matters for content creation, and describe three specific elements that should be standardized in a visual brand system.
questions.answer: Visual brand consistency matters because it builds recognition in crowded feeds, allowing audiences to identify your content before reading any text. This builds trust through professionalism and familiarity, and it compounds over time into visual brand equity. Three elements to standardize: (1) Color palette: primary, secondary, and accent colors used consistently across all content. (2) Typography: specific font pairings for headings and body text, with consistent sizing hierarchy. (3) Layout patterns: consistent spacing, alignment, and composition rules that create a recognizable visual rhythm across different content formats.

rubric.key: Explains why consistency matters with clear reasoning
Describes what standardization means for each element

questions.question: Describe the four-part script structure for a 30-60 second social media video and explain why the hook must be accomplished in 3 seconds or less.
questions.answer: The four-part structure is: (1) Hook (0-3 seconds): a visual or verbal element that stops the scroll, such as a bold text overlay, surprising visual, or provocative question. (2) Context (3-10 seconds): briefly establishing what the video is about and why the viewer should care. (3) Body (10-45 seconds): delivering the main content, whether educational, entertaining, or persuasive. (4) CTA (final 5 seconds): telling the viewer what to do next (follow, comment, visit link). The hook must happen in 3 seconds because social platforms auto-play video in the feed and users make instant scroll-or-watch decisions. Platform data consistently shows that if a video does not capture attention within the first 3 seconds, the majority of viewers scroll past.

rubric.key: Lists all four structural parts correctly
Connects to viewer behavior and platform mechanics

questions.question: What is content atomization, and provide an example of how a 1500-word blog post could be atomized into at least five distinct content pieces across different platforms.
questions.answer: Content atomization is the systematic process of breaking one long-form content asset into multiple smaller platform-specific pieces, each standing alone while linking back to the original. From a 1500-word blog post, five atomized pieces could be: (1) An Instagram carousel summarizing the five key points with visual slides. (2) A Twitter/X thread pulling out the most quotable insights with commentary. (3) A LinkedIn post sharing the article's main argument with a professional perspective and link. (4) A 60-second video summarizing the article's core message for TikTok or Reels. (5) An email newsletter section featuring the article's most surprising finding as a teaser with a link to the full post.

rubric.key: Each piece is adapted for its platform rather than simply reposted

major_assignments.title: Content Campaign: From Pillar to Platform
major_assignments.description: Create a complete content campaign starting from one pillar content piece and atomizing it into platform-specific content across five channels. The campaign must include a written pillar, visual assets, video content, and a distribution plan. All content must follow a unified brand system and storytelling framework.
objectives: Produce a high-quality pillar content piece demonstrating advanced writing and storytelling
Atomize the pillar into platform-native content for five different channels
Design and apply a visual brand system consistently across all content
Develop and document a distribution strategy with timeline and channel allocation
deliverables: Pillar content piece (1500-2000 words) published on a blog platform or Google Doc
Instagram carousel (8-10 slides) designed with branded templates
Short-form video (30-60 seconds) scripted, filmed, and edited for TikTok/Reels
LinkedIn article or post (400-600 words) adapted from the pillar with professional framing
Email newsletter issue featuring the pillar content with subject line and preview text
Distribution plan documenting channel allocation, timeline, and amplification strategy
Visual brand guide (1-2 pages) documenting the system used across all content

criteria: Content is well-researched, original, and provides genuine value
Storytelling framework is applied effectively
Writing is clear, engaging, and appropriate for the target audience
SEO best practices are applied (heading structure, keyword integration)
Each platform piece is genuinely adapted, not just reformatted
Platform norms for format, length, and tone are respected
Visual brand system is applied consistently across all pieces
Video content meets production quality standards from the course
Distribution plan covers owned, earned, and paid channels
Timeline is realistic and strategically sequenced
Target audiences and objectives are defined for each channel
Amplification tactics are specific and actionable
Brand guide is clear and comprehensive
Visual system is applied consistently across all deliverables
Typography, color, and layout choices are professional and cohesive
Brand system enhances content recognition and professionalism
Exceptional pillar content demonstrating advanced storytelling and writing
Skillful atomization showing platform expertise across five channels
Comprehensive distribution strategy with clear logic and timeline
Cohesive visual brand system tying all content together professionally

quizzes.description: Regular quizzes on content creation concepts and frameworks

weekly_production_exercises.description: Weekly content production exercises across formats with peer review

major_assignment.description: Comprehensive content campaign from pillar to platform

midterm_portfolio.description: Mid-semester portfolio showcasing best production work and growth

final_portfolio.description: Final portfolio demonstrating mastery across all content formats

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all production sessions. More than 2 unexcused absences from lab sessions result in a half-letter grade reduction. Lecture attendance is strongly expected.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. Production exercises submitted more than 3 days late receive no credit. Portfolio deadlines are firm.
course_policies.collaboration: Production exercises may involve team shoots and peer feedback. All graded submissions must represent individual work. Sharing project files, templates, or raw footage between students is prohibited.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All content must be original. Stock media must be properly licensed and credited. AI-generated images, text, or video must be disclosed. Submitting content produced for another course is self-plagiarism.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need a smartphone capable of 1080p video, CapCut or DaVinci Resolve for editing, Canva Pro (free student trial), and a blog platform (Medium, Substack, or WordPress). Microphones and lighting equipment are available for checkout from the media lab.
